chunk_summary: This section discusses the escalation from dietary assessment to the use of supplements and enteral tube feeding, focusing on severe acute malnutrition (SAM) and pediatric feeding disorders.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for escalating to enteral tube feeding in children with severe acute malnutrition.
Learn how to assess appetite and feeding skills in pediatric patients.
Identify key guidelines for managing pediatric feeding disorders.
Familiarize with WHO recommendations for inpatient care of children aged 6–59 months with SAM.
key_facts: Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in children aged 6–59 months may require inpatient care as per WHO guidelines.
Appetite assessment is a critical step in managing SAM and determining the need for enteral feeding.
Enteral tube feeding is recommended when oral intake is insufficient to meet nutritional needs.
Pediatric feeding disorders are defined as impaired oral intake that is not age-appropriate and is associated with medical, nutritional, or psychosocial dysfunction.
The WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children provides standardized guidelines for managing common childhood illnesses, including malnutrition.
reference_quote: Severe acute malnutrition in children aged 6–59 months may require inpatient care as per WHO guidelines — https://www.who.int/tools/elena/interventions/sam-identification-inpatient.
